front: What is the primary purpose of diagnostic checks applied to residuals in time series analysis?
back: The primary purpose is to validate the assumptions of the fitted model (e.g., an ARIMA model). The goal is to ensure the residuals resemble white noise—uncorrelated, normally distributed, and with a constant mean of zero and constant variance.

front: What is the most common test for autocorrelation in residuals and what is its null hypothesis?
back: The Ljung-Box test (Q-test) is the most common. Its null hypothesis (H₀) is that the residuals are independently distributed, meaning there is no autocorrelation in the residual series up to the specified lag.

front: How can you visually check the assumption of no autocorrelation in residuals?
back: By examining the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) plot of the residuals. For a good model, no autocorrelation coefficient should be significantly different from zero (i.e., all spikes should lie within the confidence bands).

front: What diagnostic plot is used to check the assumption of constant variance (homoscedasticity) in residuals?
back: A time plot of the residuals. The spread of the residuals should be constant over time, without any visible patterns, trends, or fan-shaped behavior (e.g., increasing variance as the forecast variable increases).

front: What is the name of the plot used to check the normality assumption of the residuals?
back: A Normal Q-Q (Quantile-Quantile) plot. If the residuals are normally distributed, the points will fall approximately along the straight reference line. Significant deviations from the line suggest the normality assumption is violated.
